# ”Big Cloud and the Fantasy of Imaginary Scale"

![](data:image/png;base64,)
"Small software project, big ambitions, zero production traffic — but somehow the plan already involves hyperscaler bureaucracy. Let’s talk about the **Big Cloud Fetish**”
date: "2026-04-25"
tags:

- cloud
- devops
- infrastructure
- aws
- cloudflare
- fly.io
- startups

---

# The Hyperscaler Fetish

There is a strange disease in software infrastructure conversations.

A company has no product in production yet. No real traffic. No mature deployment pipeline. No operational team. No customer scale. No existing cloud investment. Sometimes not even a finished backend.

And then someone says the magic words:

> “But we should use AWS, because we want to scale.”

This sentence has probably burned more startup engineering hours than bad ORM defaults.

And I get it. I really do. AWS sounds serious. AWS sounds enterprise. AWS sounds like the responsible adult in the room. It gives people the feeling that the company is preparing for success.

But that is exactly the problem.

A lot of infrastructure decisions are not made from technical need. They are made from **infrastructure aesthetics**. AWS feels like scale. Kubernetes feels like scale. Multi-account cloud strategy feels like scale. Private subnets, IAM boundaries, Terraform modules, service meshes, event buses, observability pipelines — all of this feels like the architecture of a company that already won.

But most companies are not there.

Most companies are still trying to discover whether the product deserves to exist.

And at that stage, choosing infrastructure because it resembles the future version of your company is usually just a very expensive form of cosplay.

Those are not the same thing.

A Formula 1 car is fast. It is also a terrible vehicle for grocery shopping.

---

## The cloud is not one thing anymore

For a long time, “cloud” basically meant AWS. Then Azure and Google Cloud became the other default names in the room.

But the world around them changed.

The choice is no longer:

```txt
Buy servers
or
Use AWS
```

There is now a serious middle category: developer-focused infrastructure platforms that are not toys, not “just hosting,” and not limited to one framework.

Cloudflare, Fly.io, Render, Railway, Koyeb, Sevalla, DigitalOcean, Scaleway, and others are building opinionated infrastructure products with modern defaults.

Some of them are PaaS-like. Some are edge-first. Some are microVM-based. Some are self-service clouds with nicer ergonomics. Some are serverless. Some are container platforms. Some are weird in a good way.

I like calling them **Cloud Tigers**.

Not because they are bigger than the hyperscalers. They are not. But because they are fast, focused, ambitious, and often much better aligned with what small and medium software teams actually need:

```txt
ship the product
keep ops low
avoid unnecessary bureaucracy
scale when needed
do not build an internal platform team cosplay
```

That last one is important.

A lot of companies do not need a hyperscaler. They need a path to production that does not punish them for being small.

---

## The hyperscaler argument is usually emotional

The argument usually sounds rational:

> “We need AWS because we plan to scale.”

But under the surface, it is often emotional.

AWS feels serious. AWS feels enterprise. AWS feels like the company is preparing for success. It creates the sensation that the business is already on the path to becoming something huge.

This is the fetish part.

The infrastructure choice becomes a symbol. AWS is treated less like a tool and more like a maturity costume.

But infrastructure should not be chosen for the imaginary future version of the company that has ten million users, a platform team, an SRE rotation, reserved instances, multi-region strategy, compliance automation, internal developer portals, and a dedicated Slack channel for IAM disasters.

It should be chosen for the company that exists now.

And the company that exists now probably needs:

```txt
git push deploys
preview environments
managed Postgres
background jobs
object storage
simple secrets
logs that are not a scavenger hunt
metrics that humans can read
low-friction rollbacks
a boring deployment story
a bill that does not require FinOps divination
```

Not a two-hundred-service buffet where every service has its own pricing page, permission model, deployment pattern, and footgun.

---

## DevOps is not supposed to be soul damage

There is also something people get wrong about DevOps.

DevOps teams are supposed to be **DevOps**, not just Ops with a cooler job title and YAML trauma.

Building an architecture stack can be fun. It can be neat. It can be creative engineering. Designing deployment flows, observability, environment strategy, service boundaries, database lifecycle, background processing, failure recovery — that is real engineering work.

But managing hyperscaler bureaucracy is different.

IAM archaeology is not product engineering. Fighting permission policies is not architecture. Spending days understanding why one cloud service can talk to another cloud service only when the correct ritual of roles, policies, trust relationships, regions, VPC settings, security groups, and service-linked whatever has been performed is not innovation.

It eats your soul.

And it costs money either way.

Either you pay the cloud bill, or you pay more salaries for people whose job is to survive the cloud bill.

Sometimes both.

Infrastructure cost is not only compute, storage, and bandwidth.

It is also:

```txt
learning curve
deployment friction
debugging time
operational surface area
security configuration burden
billing unpredictability
vendor-specific knowledge
team cognitive load
time not spent on product
```

A startup does not only run out of money.

It runs out of attention.

Every hour spent debugging IAM permissions for a service the team barely understands is an hour not spent improving onboarding, fixing product gaps, talking to users, reducing churn, building core domain logic, or making the software actually valuable.

This is why “AWS is cheaper at scale” can be a misleading argument.

At what scale?

With what team?

With what workload?

With what reserved capacity?

With what operational maturity?

With how many engineers quietly becoming infrastructure maintainers?

A small team choosing AWS for theoretical future scale may be buying negative velocity today in exchange for an imaginary discount tomorrow.

That is bad finance and worse engineering.

---

## AWS deserves respect — and skepticism

I respect AWS.

No serious person should write about cloud infrastructure while pretending AWS is incompetent. AWS created the default mental model for modern cloud. It is massive, mature, battle-tested, and it can support workloads most companies will never come close to having.

AWS is, in many ways, **the cloud**.

But this is also why AWS is dangerous for early teams.

AWS is no longer just an infrastructure product. It feels more like a **raw computing civilization**.

It gives you everything. But “everything” is not the same as a good product experience.

This is where I think the AWS fetish becomes obvious. There are entire companies that use AWS as the underlying compute layer and then build better infrastructure products on top of it.

That is not random.

That is a market signal.

If AWS-native products were always the best way to consume AWS primitives, half of this platform ecosystem would not need to exist.

AWS has products for everything: containers, functions, queues, databases, object storage, workflows, APIs, logs, identity, networking, deployment, secrets, monitoring, and probably three different services for whatever word you just thought of.

But the experience often feels like walking through sedimentary layers of Amazon history.

Powerful? Yes.

Elegant? Not usually.

ECS is a good example. It is simpler than Kubernetes, and for many AWS-native teams it is a reasonable choice. But it is still proprietary orchestration complexity. It is complex enough that you need to learn it seriously.

And once I need to learn proprietary orchestration anyway, I start asking:

> Why am I learning this instead of using Kubernetes, Nomad, Fly Machines, or a higher-level platform that removes most of this concern entirely?

This is the uncomfortable truth:

You are not receiving the benefits of being Netflix.

You are receiving the homework of someone who wants to look like they might become Netflix one day.

---

## Azure: the enterprise default nobody loves out loud

Azure is harder to talk about because Azure’s power is not developer love.

It is distribution.

Azure wins because companies already live inside Microsoft. Identity, enterprise contracts, procurement, compliance, Active Directory, Office, Teams, SharePoint, Power BI, Dynamics, Windows Server history — Azure enters through the side door labeled:

> “We already pay Microsoft.”

That is a valid business advantage.

Sometimes that is exactly the right answer.

But if you are a small team choosing Azure from scratch because you think it is the best developer experience for a greenfield product, I have questions.

Azure often feels like infrastructure designed by a committee that was interrupted by another committee. There are strong services. There are serious enterprise capabilities. But the product experience is rarely the cleanest path from idea to production.

Azure is excellent when the organization context demands Azure.

It is rarely the best answer when the only requirement is:

> “We need to ship fast and not drown.”

---

## Google Cloud: the genius with an identity crisis

Google Cloud is the big cloud I find the most interesting, because you can see the taste trying to escape.

GCP has good ideas. Cloud Run is one of the best mainstream container deployment experiences. BigQuery is excellent. Google’s Kubernetes heritage is real. Some parts of the platform feel modern, clean, and developer-aware.

And then, suddenly, you are back inside big-cloud bureaucracy.

That is the strange thing about GCP. It often feels like two companies fighting each other.

One company wants to build an elegant developer platform.

The other wants to be a hyperscaler with all the enterprise knobs, IAM ceremony, product overlap, quota weirdness, org policies, and documentation archaeology that come with that ambition.

Google Cloud should be the opinionated hyperscaler. It should be the one that says:

> “We know the modern path. Here it is.”

Instead, it sometimes feels undecided between being the infrastructure platform of the future and being “AWS, but with Google colors.”

And that is probably part of why Azure, despite being much less exciting from a developer taste perspective, is still ahead of it.

Azure has distribution. Azure has Microsoft gravity. Azure has enterprise lock-in as a superpower.

GCP has better taste in many places, but it does not always have a clear enough stance.

---

## The Cloud Tigers

The more interesting infrastructure story is happening below the hyperscaler layer.

Not because the smaller players are universally better.

They are not.

They have trade-offs, limits, outages, missing features, regional constraints, support differences, and sometimes pricing surprises of their own.

But they usually start from a different question.

The hyperscaler asks:

> “What primitive can we expose?”

The Cloud Tiger asks:

> “What experience should the developer have?”

That changes everything.

---

## Cloudflare: pick a side

Cloudflare is probably the strangest and most interesting player in this entire conversation.

And I should be honest here:

Cloudflare is not a neutral cloud. It is not trying to give you the same mental model as AWS with a nicer dashboard. It has its own concepts, its own runtime model, its own edge-first worldview, and its own collection of primitives that can feel weird until they click.

Workers are not just tiny Node servers.

Durable Objects are not Redis with a Cloudflare sticker. They are closer to stateful actors that give you a coordination point for a specific room, user, document, session, game, or workflow.

That is a very different mental model.

R2 is not just S3 with orange paint.

D1 is not just “SQLite but cloud.”

The whole platform has its own physics.

And yes, sometimes the physics are strange.

But this is product-strange, not bureaucracy-strange.

That distinction matters.

AWS weirdness often feels like paperwork. Cloudflare weirdness feels like design philosophy. You may agree with the philosophy or not, but at least there is one.

Cloudflare wants you to build in a specific way: globally distributed, edge-aware, event-driven, latency-sensitive, with managed coordination primitives and a runtime that does not look like the traditional “server in a region behind a load balancer” model.

That is why I jokingly call Cloudflare the Apple of cloud infrastructure.

Not because the branding is bad.

That was the joke.

Cloudflare has a strong brand. It is just not Apple-level “the whole thing feels like one polished object” yet.

The Apple comparison is more about the product posture.

Cloudflare says:

> “Do it our way.”

Which can be annoying.

But the important difference is: their way is often good.

Sometimes extremely good.

Sometimes better than the traditional cloud model by a lot.

This is what makes Cloudflare so interesting. It is proprietary. It is opinionated. It has weird primitives. It can absolutely lock you into its worldview.

But the worldview is not random.

It is coherent. It is modern. It is built around the idea that global infrastructure should be accessible without assembling a private distributed systems department.

That is rare.

So yes, I am biased. I like Cloudflare, people call me a Workers Maniac. I like when infrastructure has taste. I like when a provider says, “Here is a better primitive,” instead of giving me a thousand knobs and calling that flexibility.

But if you use Cloudflare, you need to accept the trade.

You are not buying generic cloud.

You are buying into a worldview.

For me, that is often worth it.

---

## Fly.io: freedom, control, and looking good while doing it

Fly.io is the conceptual one.

I do not mean that in a shallow landing-page way. I mean Fly has one of the best conceptual models in modern infrastructure:

> Run real applications, inside Firecracker microVMs, close to users, with a strong global networking layer.

That is just cool.

Fly gives me something I care about a lot: **freedom with low ops**.

I do not want to be trapped inside a narrow platform where everything is magical until the moment I need to do something slightly outside the expected path.

But I also do not want to spend my life assembling cloud primitives like IKEA furniture from hell.

Fly hits a very interesting middle point.

It gives you machines. Real machine-like things. Your app runs in a container. You get global placement. You get private networking. You get enough control to feel like an adult, but not so much ceremony that you need a platform team just to deploy a backend.

That is the sweet spot.

It is lower-level than Render or Railway. It is less “click here and forget.” But it is also far less bureaucratic than hyperscaler infrastructure.

You can understand the model.

You can reason about it.

You can build serious always-on services without immediately falling into Kubernetes-shaped complexity.

Fly gives you that feeling.

It feels like infrastructure designed by people who still think computers are cool.

That matters more than people admit.

Because when a platform has taste, your own architecture tends to get better. When the primitives are clean, your system design has fewer excuses to become ugly.

Fly is not perfect. No provider is. But a highly. conceptual, badass and innovative model

> Here you want to deploy with low ops, Edge friendly Firecracker microVMs. And you want to look sexy while doing it.
>
> But conceptually, it is one of the strongest alternatives to the hyperscaler default.

---

## Railway, Render, Koyeb, Sevalla: Heroku was right

People forget how magical Heroku felt.

```sh
git push heroku main
```

That was not just a deployment command.

It was a statement:

> Developers should not need to become infrastructure monks to ship software.

Heroku was right.

The market just spent years rediscovering that after everyone collectively decided that YAML, Kubernetes, Terraform, sidecars, service meshes, and cloud IAM would somehow make us happier.

Railway, Render, Koyeb, Sevalla, and similar platforms are part of that correction.

They say:

```txt
connect your repo
configure your services
deploy your app
add a database
add environment variables
scale when needed
move on
```

That is not childish.

That is not unserious.

That is exactly what many teams need.

---

### Railway

Railway leans hardest into DX.

It is fast, friendly, template-driven, and almost aggressively optimized for getting from idea to running system. It feels like a platform built by people who understand that early software work is exploratory.

You do not always know what the architecture should be.

You are discovering it.

Railway is very good for that phase.

The risk is that convenience can blur boundaries. You need to keep an eye on usage and structure. But that is a much better problem than spending three weeks designing AWS networking for a product nobody has used yet.

Railway gives you momentum.

And momentum is underrated.

---

### Render

Render is probably the cleanest “modern Heroku” mental model.

Web services, workers, cron jobs, private services, managed databases, Git deploys, preview environments — it has the pieces most teams need without making every deployment feel like a cloud certification exam.

Render is not as conceptually wild as Cloudflare or Fly.

That is fine.

Its value is being boring in the right way.

There is a category of product where I do not want edge-native state coordination or microVM placement strategy. I just want a backend, a worker, a database, logs, deploys, and sleep.

Render is good for that.

Beware cold starts depending on tier and service shape, especially if you are expecting always-warm behavior. But as a platform model, it is solid.

---

### Koyeb

Koyeb is interesting because it feels like a more global, container-native, serverless-flavored PaaS.

It supports Git and Docker deployments, autoscaling, scale-to-zero, services, and a clean flow for running apps without owning the orchestration layer.

It sits in a nice position: more explicit runtime flexibility than some simple PaaS offerings, but still much less operational burden than hyperscaler setups.

Koyeb is the kind of provider I would look at when I want modern app hosting, containers, global deployment options, and a lighter operational model.

Not revolutionary in the Cloudflare or Fly sense, but very relevant.

---

### Sevalla

Sevalla is newer, from the Kinsta team, and it feels like a managed hosting company expanding into broader application infrastructure.

That is not a bad thing.

Kinsta understands managed web operations. Sevalla seems to take that sensibility and apply it to applications, databases, static sites, CDN integration, Git-based deploys, Docker support, and building-block-style infrastructure.

It does not radically change the category.

It is more like:

> Here is another clean, modern, high-DX platform from a team that understands hosting.

That is valid.

Not every provider needs to reinvent computing. Sometimes the win is just removing enough friction that the team keeps shipping.

---

## DigitalOcean and Scaleway: self-service without enterprise theater

Not every team wants a PaaS.

Sometimes you want a more self-service cloud. You want VMs, managed databases, object storage, Kubernetes if necessary, networking, and normal infrastructure primitives — but without the feeling that you accidentally joined a Fortune 500 cloud migration program.

This is where DigitalOcean and Scaleway are very strong.

---

### DigitalOcean

DigitalOcean has always understood developers better than most big clouds.

It is not the newest or flashiest option, but it has a very clear value: familiar infrastructure, lower ceremony, approachable product experience.

Droplets, managed databases, App Platform, Kubernetes, Spaces, load balancers — nothing here is conceptually confusing.

That is the point.

DigitalOcean is good when you want something closer to traditional cloud infrastructure but do not want hyperscaler bureaucracy.

It is the:

> “Give me servers and managed services without making me hate computers.”

option.

For many companies, that is enough.

Actually, it is more than enough.

---

### Scaleway

Scaleway is excellent.

It deserves more attention than it gets, especially if you care about European infrastructure, sovereignty, and avoiding the big three without falling back to some tiny VPS provider with questionable durability.

Scaleway has a real cloud portfolio: compute, managed databases, Kubernetes, object storage, serverless containers, serverless functions, AI infrastructure, networking, and more.

It is not just “cheap servers in Europe.”

And the European angle matters.

Data sovereignty is not only a buzzword anymore. It is becoming a real architectural and procurement concern. For companies operating in Europe, serving European customers, or wanting a provider outside the US hyperscaler triangle, Scaleway is one of the most compelling options.

It has enough primitives to build serious systems, but it still feels more approachable than the big cloud maze.

For self-service infrastructure with a European stance, Scaleway is one of the best names in the conversation.

---

## The migration fear is overblown

Another common argument:

> “If we start outside AWS, migrating later will be hard.”

Yes.

Migration is work.

But early over-engineering is also work.

Usually more work.

And unlike migration work, it happens before the product has proved it deserves the complexity.

Also, you can make migration easier without starting on a hyperscaler:

```txt
use containers
keep state in boring databases
avoid vendor-specific business logic
keep queues abstracted behind small interfaces
use S3-compatible object storage when possible
keep infrastructure definitions readable
avoid turning platform-specific features into your domain model
separate application architecture from deployment architecture
```

A well-designed app on Fly, Render, Railway, Koyeb, DigitalOcean, Scaleway, or Cloudflare can be migrated later if the business earns that problem.

But a badly designed app on AWS is not magically portable because the logo was enterprise-approved.

Starting on AWS does not prevent lock-in.

It often accelerates it.

IAM, Lambda, Step Functions, DynamoDB, EventBridge, SQS, SNS, Cognito, API Gateway, CloudWatch, CDK, ECS, EKS, Fargate — these are powerful services, but they are not neutral abstractions.

They shape your system.

Sometimes that is fine.

But do not pretend the hyperscaler path is the anti-lock-in path.

It is usually just lock-in with better enterprise procurement.

---

## When hyperscalers are the right answer

This article is not an argument that nobody should use AWS, GCP, or Azure.

Hyperscalers make sense when you actually need hyperscaler-shaped things:

```txt
large enterprise contracts
strict customer procurement requirements
deep compliance programs
existing cloud commitments
complex private networking
specialized managed services
massive data platforms
advanced IAM/org structures
global multi-region enterprise workloads
GPU/AI capacity at serious scale
tight integration with Microsoft, Google, or Amazon ecosystems
a team that already knows the platform well
```

AWS is a very good answer when the organization already has AWS expertise, AWS commitments, AWS security controls, AWS landing zones, AWS observability, and AWS operational muscle.

Azure is a very good answer when the company is deeply Microsoft-native.

GCP is a very good answer when the workload fits its strengths: data, analytics, Cloud Run, GKE, BigQuery, Pub/Sub, ML tooling, and Google’s network.

The mistake is not choosing a hyperscaler.

The mistake is choosing a hyperscaler because it feels adult.

---

## A better rule

Here is the rule I wish more teams followed:

Not your imaginary IPO architecture.

Not your “what if we become Uber?” architecture.

Not your “I watched three re:Invent talks and now everything is an event-driven multi-account landing zone” architecture.

Your next 18 months.

For many teams, that means:

```txt
Cloudflare for edge-native, globally distributed, event-driven systems.

Fly.io for globally deployed containers and always-on services with great networking.

Render, Railway, Koyeb, or Sevalla for high-DX app hosting and managed services.

DigitalOcean for a clean self-service cloud with familiar primitives.

Scaleway for European cloud, sovereignty, and a serious alternative cloud portfolio.

AWS, GCP, or Azure when their specific strengths or organizational requirements are real.
```

That is not anti-cloud.

That is pro-fit.

---

## The point of infrastructure is not infrastructure

The point of infrastructure is to let the product exist.

Users do not care if your startup has a beautiful Terraform module layout. They do not care if your ECS cluster is properly named. They do not care if your private subnets look good in a diagram. They do not care if your IAM policies express the deep sadness of least privilege.

They care that the product works.

They care that it is fast enough, reliable enough, secure enough, and improving fast enough.

For a young company, infrastructure should create leverage.

If it creates drag, it is the wrong infrastructure — even if it is “industry standard.”

The hyperscalers are incredible.

They are also often too much machine for the job.

And the funny thing is: choosing a smaller, simpler, higher-DX provider is not anti-scale. It is often the most scalable decision a team can make, because the first thing that needs to scale is not your Kubernetes cluster.

It is your ability to ship.

So yes, respect AWS.

Respect GCP.

Respect Azure. (maybe not as much)

But do not worship them.
